My closest friend for 30 years+ caught a cold in November. Now we used to talk 3 or 4 times a week. By January he could hardly speak, other than croak like a frog. That's when I stepped in & hit his GP & the head of Dorset's NHS service with two emails & he had a doctor's appointment within 48hrs, otherwise impossible. He couldn't get an appointment but, from 3,500 miles away, I made it happen & shook the tree as high as I could go & it was all in vain...by September the next year he was dead. Throat cancer & it all began with what seemed to be a cold or flu......

'Excuse me, you're in the shot,' a woman barks at me as I shuffle past. No, I'm not trying to get onto a rush-hour bus, I'm simply attempting to enter my own home. She's adorned with four empty Harrods bags, stilettos and a tweed mini dress, which I spy a price tag hanging out the back of. EXCLUSIVEBrit is arrested, thrown in a Moroccan jail cell and left without any way of contacting his family 'after filming himself helping hungry children'

NEW When Sam Rushton saved up donations to help hungry children in Morocco, he never imagined his act of kindness would land him in a prison cell. But that is exactly what happened to the 20-year-old from Ashton-under-Lyne, who was detained by foreign authorities, held in jail for several hours, and had his passport and phone seized for days. Mr Rushton, a carer, was arrested after filming himself handing out basic supplies to local children in Marrakesh using money he had raised through social media.

It's disheartening to see the same pattern repeat: the skeptics who once insisted there was 'nothing' beneath the pyramids, who downplay the profound implications of Göbekli Tepe, and who now approach new discoveries with the same dismissive certainty. Too often it stems from an unspoken belief that modern humanity already sits at the peak of achievement—leaving little room for humility, curiosity, or genuine openness to evidence that challenges the established timeline. True progress requires staying willing to question what we think we know.

Is it not ironic that here we have a disabled fellow wanting to work and yet we have thousands who only claim to have a disability and don't want to work.

Bryan Rowe, 21, said he had been left feeling like he is 'not good enough' for his dream job after his local council turned him down. Mr Rowe suffers with myotonic dystrophy (DM) and ADHD, but worked as a waste management loader for nine months as part of his supported internship. Myotonic dystrophy is a form of muscular dystrophy which affects muscles and organs in the body, causing progressive muscle degeneration with weakness and shrinkage of muscle tissue. After completing the unpaid role, further job opportunities came up and he was encouraged to apply, but was left 'broken-hearted' after being rejected twice. His mother, Claire Rowe, described how her son, from Sholing in Southampton, Hants, had been obsessed with being a bin man from the age of two.
  • 136 comments
  • share Disabled man heartbroken after two rejections from dream job as binman

Based on the best-selling sci-fi novel by Andy Weird, the movie stars Ryan Gosling as teacher-turned-astronaut, Ryland Grace, who is sent into space to save the Earth after the sun starts dimming. During his time in space, Grace forms an unlikely friendship with an alien called Rocky, who proves to be a vital part of the Earth-saving mission. While the movie has rocketed to the top of the box office this week, Dr Jacqueline McCleary, an observational cosmologist at Northeastern University, says it has several flaws.

My friends and I used Pan-Stik as lipstick because it was hard to find a pale shade in the early sixties. Max Factor was then considered expensive, and we mostly bought brands from Woolworth - Miners, and Outdoor Girl. Mascara was much thicker if you used saliva rather than water (revolting but true).

While some nostalgic items like Yardley's sticky 'slickers' glosses are long gone, you can get your hands on Revlon's icy raspberry 'Cherries in the Snow' lipstick and Max Factor's trusty 'Pan Stik'.
